title: JavaScript Concepts
response:
Arrow functions (=>) are a shorter syntax for writing functions, introduced in ES6. Key differences from regular functions:

1. Concise syntax — single-expression body can omit braces and return.
2. No own this — they inherit this from the surrounding lexical scope. This is the most important behavioural difference.
3. No arguments object — use rest parameters instead.
4. Cannot be used as constructors — no new keyword.
5. No prototype property.

Use arrow functions for callbacks and short helpers. Use regular functions when you need their own this (e.g., object methods, constructors).
